volumeTitle: Children of Heracles. Hippolytus. Andromache. Hecuba
label: Children of Heracles (Section 1 - Section 160)

sections.label: Section 1
sections.original: Πάλαι ποτʼ ἐστὶ τοῦτʼ ἐμοὶ δεδογμένον ·
sections.translation: I hold this true , and long have held : Nature hath made one man upright for his neighbours ’ good , while another hath a disposition wholly given over to gain , useless alike to the state and difficult to have dealings with ,

sections.label: Section 5
sections.original: αὑτῷ δʼ ἄριστος · οἶδα δʼ οὐ λόγῳ μαθών .
sections.translation: but for himself the best of men ; and this I know , not from mere hearsay . I , for instance , from pure regard and reverence for my kith and kin , though I might have lived at peace in Argos , alone of all my race shared with Heracles his labours , while he was yet with us , and now that he dwells in heaven ,

sections.label: Section 10
sections.original: ναίει , τὰ κείνου τέκνʼ ἔχων ὑπὸ πτεροῖς
sections.translation: I keep these his children safe beneath my wing , though myself I need protection . For when their father passed from earth away , Eurystheus would first of all have slain us , but we escaped . And though our home is lost , our life was saved . But in exile we wander from city to city ,

sections.label: Section 15
sections.original: ψυχὴ δʼ ἐσώθη . φεύγομεν δʼ ἀλώμενοι
sections.translation: ever forced to roam . For , added to our former wrongs , Eurystheus thought it fit to put this further outrage upon us : wheresoe ’ er he heard that we were settling , thither would he send heralds

sections.label: Section 20
sections.original: κήρυκας ἐξαιτεῖ τε κἀξείργει χθονός ,
sections.translation: demanding our surrender and driving us from thence , holding [*] out this threat , that Argos is no mean city to make a friend or foe , and furthermore pointing to his own prosperity . So they , seeing how weak my means , and these little ones left without a father ,

sections.label: Section 25
sections.original: τοὺς κρείσσονας σέβοντες ἐξείργουσι γῆς .
sections.translation: bow to his superior might and drive us from their land . And I share the exile of these children , and help them bear their evil lot by my sympathy , loth to betray them , lest someone say , Look you ! now that the children ’ s sire is dead ,

sections.label: Section 30
sections.original: Ἰόλαος οὐκ ἤμυνε συγγενὴς γεγώς .
sections.translation: Iolaus no more protects them , kinsman though he is . Not one corner left us in the whole of Hellas , we are come to Marathon and its neighbouring land , and here we sit as suppliants at the altars of the gods , and pray their aid ; for ’ tis said

sections.label: Section 35
sections.original: δισσοὺς κατοικεῖν Θησέως παῖδας λόγος ,
sections.translation: two sons of Theseus dwell upon these plains , the lot of their inheritance , scions of Pandion ’ s stock , related to these children ; this the reason we have come on this our way to the borders of glorious Athens . To lead the flight two aged guides are we ;

sections.label: Section 40
sections.original: ἐγὼ μὲν ἀμφὶ τοῖσδε καλχαίνων τέκνοις ,
sections.translation: my care is centred on these boys , while she , I mean Alcmena , clasps her son ’ s daughter in her arms , and bears her for safety within this shrine , for we shrink from letting tender maidens [*] come anigh the crowd or stand as suppliants at the altar .

sections.label: Section 45
sections.original: Ὕλλος δʼ ἀδελφοί θʼ οἷσι πρεσβεύει γένος
sections.translation: Now Hyllus and the elder of his brethren are seeking some place for us to find a refuge , if we are driven by force from this land .

sections.label: Section 48
sections.original: ὦ τέκνα τέκνα , δεῦρο , λαμβάνεσθʼ ἐμῶν
sections.translation: O children , children , come hither ! hold unto my robe ; for lo ! I see a herald coming towards us from Eurystheus ,

sections.label: Section 50
sections.original: στείχοντʼ ἐφʼ ἡμᾶς , οὗ διωκόμεσθʼ ὕπο
sections.translation: by whom we are persecuted , wanderers excluded from every land . A curse on thee and him that sent thee , hateful wretch ! for that same tongue of thine hath oft announced its master ’ s evil hests to these children ’ s noble sire as well .

sections.label: Section 55
sections.original: ἦ που καθῆσθαι τήνδʼ ἕδραν καλὴν δοκεῖς
sections.translation: Doubtless thy folly lets thee think this is a good position to have taken up , and that thou art come to a city that will help thee . No ! there is none that will prefer thy feeble arm to the might of Eurystheus . Begone ! why take this trouble ? Thou must arise and go

sections.label: Section 60
sections.original: ἐς Ἄργος , οὗ σε λεύσιμος μένει δίκη .
sections.translation: to Argos , where awaits thee death by stoning .

sections.label: Section 61
sections.original: οὐ δῆτʼ , ἐπεί μοι βωμὸς ἀρκέσει θεοῦ ,
sections.translation: Not so , for the god ’ s altar will protect me , and this land of freedom , wherein we have set foot .

sections.label: Section 63
sections.original: βούλῃ πόνον μοι τῇδε προσθεῖναι χερί ;
sections.translation: Wilt give me the trouble of laying hands on thee ?

sections.label: Section 64
sections.original: οὔτοι βίᾳ γέ μʼ οὐδὲ τούσδʼ ἄξεις λαβών .
sections.translation: By force at least shalt thou never drag these children hence .

sections.label: Section 65
sections.original: γνώσῃ σύ · μάντις δʼ ἦσθʼ ἄρʼ οὐ καλὸς τάδε .
sections.translation: That shalt thou soon learn ; it seems thou wert a poor prophet , after all , in this . [ Copreus here seizes the children .

sections.label: Section 66
sections.original: οὐκ ἂν γένοιτο τοῦτʼ ἐμοῦ ζῶντός ποτε .
sections.translation: This shall never happen while I live .

sections.label: Section 67
sections.original: ἄπαιρʼ · ἐγὼ δὲ τούσδε , κἂν σὺ μὴ θέλῃς ,
sections.translation: Begone ! for I will take them hence , for all thy refusals , for I hold [*] that they belong to Eurystheus , as they do indeed .

sections.label: Section 69
sections.original: ὦ τὰς Ἀθήνας δαρὸν οἰκοῦντες χρόνον ,
sections.translation: Help , ye who long nave had your home in Athens !

sections.label: Section 70
sections.original: ἀμύνεθʼ · ἱκέται δʼ ὄντες ἀγοραίου Διὸς
sections.translation: we suppliants at Zeus ’ altar in your market-place are being haled by force away , our sacred wreaths defiled , shame to your city , to the gods dishonour .

sections.label: Section 73
sections.original: — ἔα ἔα · τίς ἡ βοὴ βωμοῦ πέλας
sections.translation: Hark , hark ! What cry is this that rises near the altar ? At once explain the nature of the trouble .

sections.label: Section 1.75
sections.original: — ἴδετε τὸν γέροντʼ ἀμαλὸν ἐπὶ πέδῳ
sections.translation: See this aged frame hurled in its feebleness upon the ground ! Woe is me !

sections.label: Section 1.77
sections.original: — πρὸς τοῦ ποτʼ ἐν γῇ πτῶμα δύστηνον πίτνεις ;
sections.translation: Who threw thee down thus pitiably ?

sections.label: Section 1.78
sections.original: ὅδʼ , ὦ ξένοι , με σοὺς ἀτιμάζων θεοὺς
sections.translation: Behold the man who flouts your gods , kind sirs , and tries by force to drag me from my seat before the altar of Zeus .

sections.label: Section 1.80
sections.original: σὺ δʼ ἐκ τίνος γῆς , ὦ γέρον , τετράπτολιν
sections.translation: From what land , old stranger , art thou come to this confederate state of four cities ? or have ye left Euboea ’ s cliffs , and , with the oar that sweeps the sea , put in here from across the firth ? [*]

sections.label: Section 1.84
sections.original: οὐ νησιώτην , ὦ ξένοι , τρίβω βίον ,
sections.translation: Sirs , no island life I lead ,

sections.label: Section 1.85
sections.original: ἀλλʼ ἐκ Μυκηνῶν σὴν ἀφίγμεθα χθόνα .
sections.translation: but from Mycenae to thy land I come .

sections.label: Section 1.86
sections.original: ὄνομα τί σε , γέρον ,
sections.translation: What do they call thee , aged sir , those folk in Mycenae ?

sections.label: Section 1.88
sections.original: τὸν Ἡράκλειον ἴστε που παραστάτην
sections.translation: Maybe ye have heard of Iolaus , the comrade of Heracles , for he was not unknown to fame .

sections.label: Section 1.90
sections.original: οἶδʼ εἰσακούσας καὶ πρίν · ἀλλὰ τοῦ ποτὲ
sections.translation: Yea , I have heard of him in bygone days ; but tell me , whose are the tender boys thou bearest in thine arms ?

sections.label: Section 1.93
sections.original: Ἡρακλέους οἵδʼ εἰσὶ παῖδες , ὦ ξένοι ,
sections.translation: These , sirs , are the sons of Heracles , come as suppliants to you and your city .

sections.label: Section 1.95
sections.original: τί χρέος ; ἢ λόγων πόλεος , ἔνεπέ μοι ,
sections.translation: What is their quest ? Are they anxious , tell me , to obtain an audience of the state ?

sections.label: Section 1.97
sections.original: μήτʼ ἐκδοθῆναι μήτε πρὸς βίαν θεῶν
sections.translation: That so they may escape surrender , nor be tom with violence from thy altars , and brought to Argos .

sections.label: Section 1.99
sections.original: ἀλλʼ οὔτι τοῖς σοῖς δεσπόταις τάδʼ ἀρκέσει ,
sections.translation: Nay , this will nowise satisfy thy masters ,

sections.label: Section 1.100
sections.original: οἳ σοῦ κρατοῦντες ἐνθάδʼ εὑρίσκουσί σε .
sections.translation: who o ’ er thee have a right , and so have tracked thee hither .

sections.label: Section 1.101
sections.original: εἰκὸς θεῶν ἱκτῆρας αἰδεῖσθαι , ξένε ,
sections.translation: Stranger , ’ tis but right we should reverence the gods ’ suppliants , suffering none with violent hand to make them [*] leave the altars , for that will dread Justice ne ’ er permit .

sections.label: Section 1.105
sections.original: ἔκπεμπέ νυν γῆς τούσδε τοὺς Εὐρυσθέως ,
sections.translation: Do thou then drive these subjects of Eurystheus forth , and this hand of mine shall abstain from violence .

sections.label: Section 1.107
sections.original: ἄθεον ἱκεσίαν
sections.translation: ’ Twere impious for the state to neglect the suppliant stranger ’ s prayer .

sections.label: Section 1.109
sections.original: καλὸν δέ γʼ ἔξω πραγμάτων ἔχειν πόδα ,
sections.translation: Yet ’ tis well to keep clear of troubles ,

sections.label: Section 1.110
sections.original: εὐβουλίας τυχόντα τῆς ἀμείνονος .
sections.translation: by adopting that counsel , which is the wiser .

sections.label: Section 111
sections.original: οὐκοῦν τυράννῳ τῆσδε γῆς φράσαντά σε
sections.translation: Thou then , shouldst have told the monarch of this land thy errand before being so bold , out of regard to his country ’ s freedom , instead of trying to drag strangers by force from the altars of the gods .

sections.label: Section 114
sections.original: τίς δʼ ἐστὶ χώρας τῆσδε καὶ πόλεως ἄναξ ;
sections.translation: Who is monarch of this land and state ?

sections.label: Section 115
sections.original: ἐσθλοῦ πατρὸς παῖς Δημοφῶν ὁ Θησέως .
sections.translation: Demophon , son of gallant Theseus .

sections.label: Section 116
sections.original: πρὸς τοῦτον ἁγὼν ἆρα τοῦδε τοῦ λόγου
sections.translation: Surely it were most to the purpose to discuss this matter somewhat with him ; all else has been said in vain .

sections.label: Section 118
sections.original: καὶ μὴν ὅδʼ αὐτὸς ἔρχεται σπουδὴν ἔχων
sections.translation: Lo ! here he comes in person , in hot haste , and Acamas his brother , to hear what thou hast to say .

sections.label: Section 120
sections.original: ἐπείπερ ἔφθης πρέσβυς ὢν νεωτέρους
sections.translation: Since thou for all thy years hast outstripped younger men in coming to the rescue to this altar of Zeus , do thou tell me what hath chanced to bring this crowd together .

sections.label: Section 123
sections.original: ἱκέται κάθηνται παῖδες οἵδʼ Ἡρακλέους
sections.translation: There sit the sons of Heracles as suppliants , having wreathed the altar , as thou seest , O king ,

sections.label: Section 125
sections.original: πατρός τε πιστὸς Ἰόλεως παραστάτης .
sections.translation: and with them is Iolaus , trusty comrade of their sire .

sections.label: Section 126
sections.original: τί δῆτʼ ἰυγμῶν ἥδʼ ἐδεῖτο συμφορά ;
sections.translation: Why should this event have called for cries of pain ?

sections.label: Section 127
sections.original: βίᾳ νιν οὗτος τῆσδʼ ἀπʼ ἐσχάρας ἄγειν
sections.translation: ( turning to Copreus ). This fellow caused the uproar by trying to drag them forcibly from this altar , and he tripped up the old man , till my tears for pity flowed .

sections.label: Section 130
sections.original: καὶ μὴν στολήν γʼ Ἕλληνα καὶ ῥυθμὸν πέπλων
sections.translation: Hellenic dress and fashion in his robes doth he no doubt adopt , but deeds like these betray the barbarian . Thou , sirrah , tell me straight the country whence thou earnest thither .

sections.label: Section 134
sections.original: Ἀργεῖός εἰμι · τοῦτο γὰρ θέλεις μαθεῖν ·
sections.translation: An Argive I ; since that thou seek ’ st to know .

sections.label: Section 135
sections.original: ἐφʼ οἷσι δʼ ἥκω καὶ παρʼ οὗ λέγειν θέλω .
sections.translation: Who sent me , and the object of my coming , will I freely tell . Eurystheus , king of Mycenae , sends me hither to fetch these back ; and I have come , sir stranger , with just grounds in plenty , alike for speech or action . An Argive myself , Argives I come to fetch ,

sections.label: Section 140
sections.original: ἐκ τῆς ἐμαυτοῦ τούσδε δραπέτας ἔχων ,
sections.translation: taking with me these runaways from my native city , on whom the doom of death was passed by our laws there ; and we have a right , since we rule our city independently , to ratify its sentences . And though they have come as suppliants to the altars of numerous others ,

sections.label: Section 145
sections.original: ἐν τοῖσιν αὐτοῖς τοισίδʼ ἕσταμεν λόγοις ,
sections.translation: we have taken our stand on these same arguments , and no one has ventured to bring upon himself evils of his own getting . But they have come hither , either because they perceived some folly in thee , or , in their perplexity , staking all on one risky throw to win or lose ;

sections.label: Section 150
sections.original: οὐ γὰρ φρενήρη γʼ ὄντα σʼ ἐλπίζουσί που
sections.translation: for surely they do not suppose that thou , if so thou hast thy senses still , and only thou , in all the breadth of Hellas they have traversed , wilt pity their foolish troubles . Come now , put argument against argument : what will be thy gain , suppose thou admit them to thy land , or let us take them hence ?

sections.label: Section 155
sections.original: τὰ μὲν παρʼ ἡμῶν τοιάδʼ ἔστι σοι λαβεῖν ,
sections.translation: From us these benefits are thine to win : this city can secure as friends Argos , with its far-reaching arm , and Eurystheus ’ might complete ; whilst if thou lend an ear to their piteous pleading and grow soft , the matter

sections.label: Section 160
sections.original: δορὸς τὸ πρᾶγμα · μὴ γὰρ ὡς μεθήσομεν
sections.translation: must result in trial of arms ; for be sure we shall not yield this struggle without appealing to the sword . What pretext wilt thou urge ? Of what domains art thou robbed that thou shouldst take and wage war with the Tirynthian Argives ? What kind of allies art thou aiding ? For [*] whom
